sales.title:
GREAT BRITAIN. Penny, ND (1024-30). Lincoln Mint. Cnut. NGC MS-64.
Pointed Helmet (BMC xiv) type; S-1158; North-787; Hild-1749; SCBI Cop-1846 (plate coin); Bruun-432. Weight: 1.14 gms. Moneyer: Swartebrand. Enchanting toning reminiscing of ancient Roman glass.
From the L. E. Bruun Collection.

Ex: Countship of Brahesminde Collection (Private Sale - 1922).
sales.description: Stack’s Bowers and Ponterio | ['October 2025 L.E. Bruun Collection']

sales.title:
Charles II, 1660-1685.
Crown 1662, London. PROOF PATTERN (GOLD OFF METAL STRIKE). CAROLVS II - DEI GRATIA Laureate bust right / MAG · BR · FRA · ET · HIB · REX Crowned cruciform shields, interlinked C's in angles. PLAIN EDGE. 58,48g. Forrer V p. 161-162 and 169; Holloway p. 45-47, n°2021; KM Pn A33; Murdoch 617; W&R. 49.
NGC PF62 (Top pop).
(2113951-008).
Splendid. The best specimen known.
The Moon, Murdoch, Hamilton Smith and C. Dabney Thomson Collections.
Glendining (13 April 1972), 372.
D. Arnold Collection, Spink 97 (13 May 1993), 36.
UBS 50 (30 June 2001), 20.
Hess-Divo 297 (22 October 2003), 77.
Dix Noonan Webb (September 2006), 636.
Kroisos Collection, Stack’s (14 January 2008), 2861.
Charles II, popularly known as the Merry Monarch, was born on the 29th of May 1630. He was the first survived son of Charles I and Henrietta Maria, the sister of the French king Louis XIII. Charles was proclaimed king of Scotland after the execution of his father in 1649 till his defeat in 1651 by Cromwell. He spent then nine years in exile in France, the Dutch Republic and the Spanish Netherlands. Following the restoration of the monarchy and the death of Cromwell, Charles was invited to return to Britain in 1658. On the 29th of May 1660, at the age of thirty he was received in London to public acclaim and acceded to the throne of England, Scotland and Ireland until his death in 1685.
Early in 1663, the ancient hand hammering process was finally superseded by the machinery of Blondeau. John and Joseph Roettier, two brothers, engraved the dies with a safeguard against clipping, the larger coins were made with an inscription on the edge. The medium-sized coins were given a grained edge. This exceptional pattern was produced for the King and the high dignitaries. The bust on obverse is different from that of circulating silver coins which have a draped bust.
sales.description: Numismatica Genevensis SA | ['Auction 22']

sales.title:
GREAT BRITAIN. Victory at the Battle of Lowestoft Silver Medal, 1665. NGC MS-62.
Eimer-230; Van Loon-526. Diameter: 62mm. Obverse: Laureate and mantled bust of Charles II right; Reverse: Charles II as a Roman General viewing a victorious British naval engagement from shore. A highly appealing and intricate naval medal featuring the scene of the battle, complete with the half-sunk wreckage of a ship, the waters around which are filled with dozens of floating survivors. The obverse rendition of the king is struck in admirably bold relief, highlighting the sculptural quality of the Roman-inspired bust, and the deeply toned surfaces lend a complementary antique nature.
sales.description: Stack’s Bowers and Ponterio | ['January 2026 NYINC Showcase Auction']

sales.title:
GREAT BRITAIN. Brass 6 Pence Pattern, 1887. Victoria. NGC MS-62.
Esc-1784a. By J. Rochelle Thomas for Spink & Sons. Obverse: Ornate bust of Queen Victoria facing left; Reverse: Crowned Royal Arms. Denomination above, date in Roman numerals below. EXTREMELY RARE, only 5-10 examples were struck in brass. Richly toned and quite attractive for the assigned grade.
Ex: Ira & Larry Goldberg Auction #41 (5/2007), Lot # 3465.
sales.description: Stack’s Bowers and Ponterio | ['Manhattan Sale']

sales.title:
GREAT BRITAIN. William Browne Prize for Classical Odes and Epigrams Bronze Medal, "1765" (after 1774). NGC MS-64 Brown.
BHM-92; Eimer-711. By Lewis Pingo. Diameter: 34.5mm. Obverse: Bust left; Reverse: Apollo, seated on a plinth at right, facing left, offers laurel crown to supplicating figure who offers a scroll.

William Browne was a London-based physician who was elected President of the College of Physicians in 1765 and served a shortened two-year term. Upon his death in 1774, he bequeathed funds to his alma mater Peterhouse, Cambridge, to establish annual gold medal prizes for poems in Latin and Greek. Awarded medals are engraved on the edge with the recipient's name and date.
From the Richard Margolis Collection.
sales.description: Stack’s Bowers and Ponterio | ['The February 2026 World Collectors Choice Online Auction (SBG)']

sales.title:
Isle of Man, Elizabeth II, Year of Planet Earth - Four Elements, Trimetallic, Crown, 2008, NGC PF68 ULTRA CAMEO
Cert Number: 4884283-001, trimetallic: gold (.999) core, titanium middle ring and gold (.999) outer ring, (4 colours titanium: brown, light blue, dark blue and purple), 38.6mm diameter, Obv: right facing uncouped bust of QEII, wearing the 'Girls of Great Britain and Ireland' tiara in the inner part, surrounded by wreath in the middle and by legend and date on outer ring, Rev: seated figures, multi-colour band, each of the four quadrants in the middle ring is a different colour of titanium and features a Greek god; Demeter for earth (brown), Zeus for air (light blue), Poseidon for water (dark blue), and Hephaestus for fire (purple), the outer ring and central circle are struck in pure gold, the outer ring depicts the 12 Signs of the zodiac to correspond to the various earth elements, whilst the central circle shows an image of the earth.
sales.description: Roxbury’s Auction House | ['Auction No. 150']

sales.title:
GREAT BRITAIN. William Browne Prize for Classical Odes and Epigrams Bronze Medal, "1765" (after 1774). NGC MS-64 Brown.
BHM-92; Eimer-711. By Lewis Pingo. Diameter: 34.5mm. Obverse: Bust of Browne facing left, perimeter legend ESSE ET VIDERI above; concentric perimeter legend D. GVLIELMVS BROWNE, EQVES. / NAT . III . NON . IAN . A . I . MDCXCII. below bust; Reverse: Apollo, seated on a plinth at right, facing left, offers laurel crown to supplicating figure who offers a scroll; perimeter legend SVNT SUA PRAEMIA LAVDI above; three-line legend ELECTVS . COLL . MED . / LOND . PRAESES . A . S . / MDCCLXV in exergue below.

William Browne was a London-based physician who was elected President of the College of Physicians in 1765 and served a shortened two-year term. Upon his death in 1774, he bequeathed funds to his alma mater Peterhouse, Cambridge, to establish annual gold medal prizes for poems in Latin and Greek. Awarded medals are engraved on the edge with the recipient's name and date.

This bronze example of the award medal offers beautiful, glossy mahogany surfaces giving this well-executed medal stunning eye appeal. Rare in bronze. Delightfully attractive.
From the Richard Margolis Collection.
To view all items from the Richard Margolis Collection, click here.
